svg.title: Derivation of the Discrete Summation Formula: an infinite weighted harmonic sum collapses into one closed-form trigonometric expression via the geometric series identity
svg.desc: Four-step diagram. Step 1: an infinite sum of harmonics with geometrically decaying amplitude a^n. Step 2: rewritten using complex exponentials via Euler's formula. Step 3: the geometric series identity collapses the infinite sum into a single fraction. Step 4: taking the imaginary part yields one closed-form equation with no summation left, which is what the DSFOscillator code evaluates directly per sample.
svg.text: 🧮 From infinite sum to one equation
The geometric series identity is the whole trick

g.text: 1️⃣ Start: infinite harmonic sum, geometrically decaying amplitude
y(θ) = Σ aⁿ · sin((n+1)θ), for n = 0 … ∞, 0 ≤ a < 1
2️⃣ Euler's formula turns sin/cos into a complex exponential
S = Σ aⁿ e^{i(n+1)θ} = e^{iθ} · Σ (a·e^{iθ})ⁿ
y(θ) is the imaginary part of S — a sum is easier to collapse in this form
3️⃣ The geometric series identity — the ENTIRE trick, one line
with r = a·e^{iθ} — an infinite sum becomes one fraction, no loop required
4️⃣ Take the imaginary part → one closed-form trig equation
✅ No summation left — this is exactly what DSF() evaluates every sample
